walkthrough.title: Choose the Board Side
walkthrough.text: Let's get Dune: Imperium Uprising set up. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. The board is two-sided. If you have 1 to 4 players, use the standard side. If you have 5 or 6 players, flip it to the expanded side with the extra board spaces. You will see a map of Arrakis with different locations connected by paths, four faction tracks along the edges, and a Conflict area in the center.
walkthrough.image_caption: Game board showing Arrakis locations and faction tracks

walkthrough.title: Place the Shield Wall
walkthrough.text: Find the Shield Wall token. It is a rectangular piece that goes below the Spice Refinery space on the board. This wall is important because it blocks sandworms from entering Conflicts at three key locations: Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, and Imperial Basin. It stays there until someone destroys it during the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Shield Wall token placed below Spice Refinery

walkthrough.title: Set Up Alliance Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now find the four Alliance tokens. Each one matches a faction: Emperor, Spacing Guild, Bene Gesserit, and Fremen. Place one Alliance token on each faction's influence track at the level 4 space. The first player to reach level 4 on a track claims that faction's Alliance token and earns a victory point.
walkthrough.image_caption: Alliance tokens placed at level 4 on faction tracks

walkthrough.title: Build the Conflict Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the Conflict cards and separate them by tier. You will see Roman numerals on the backs: I, II, and III. Shuffle each tier separately. Now stack them: put 4 Conflict III cards on the bottom, then 5 Conflict II cards on top of those, and finally 1 Conflict I card on the very top. Place this deck face down on the Conflict area of the board. The game starts with easy conflicts and escalates to high-stakes battles.
walkthrough.image_caption: Conflict deck stacked by tier with Conflict I on top

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Imperium Market
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the large Imperium deck. This is the biggest deck in the game with 69 cards. Deal 5 cards face up in a row next to the board. This is the Imperium Row, which is the market where you will buy new cards for your deck during the game. Place the rest of the Imperium deck face down beside the row.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five face-up cards in the Imperium Row

walkthrough.title: Set Out Reserve and Intrigue Decks
walkthrough.text: Place the two Reserve card stacks next to the Imperium Row. These are Prepare the Way and The Spice Must Flow. Unlike the market row, Reserve cards are always available to buy. Then shuffle the Intrigue deck and place it face down nearby. You will draw Intrigue cards during the game but keep them secret from other players.
walkthrough.image_caption: Reserve stacks and Intrigue deck beside the market

walkthrough.title: Set Out Resource Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place all the resource tokens in easy-to-reach piles near the board. You have three types: spice tokens are the orange ones, Solari tokens look like gold coins, and water tokens are blue. These form the general supply. Also set the Sandworm tokens and Maker Hook tokens nearby. You will not need them right away, but they will come into play once players start exploring the desert.
walkthrough.image_caption: Spice, Solari, water, sandworms, and Maker Hooks in the supply

walkthrough.title: Choose Leaders
walkthrough.text: Now each player picks a Leader. Spread out the 9 Leader cards and let everyone choose one. No two players can pick Leaders from the same House. Each Leader card is two-sided with different abilities, so pick the side that appeals to you. Read both the main ability on the left and the Signet Ring ability on the right. Your Leader's abilities will shape your strategy for the entire game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Leader cards spread out for selection

walkthrough.title: Take Your Starting Deck and Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes an identical 10-card starting deck. The cards are: 2 Convincing Argument, 2 Dagger, 1 Diplomacy, 2 Dune the Desert Planet, 1 Reconnaissance, 1 Seek Allies, and 1 Signet Ring. Shuffle your deck and draw 5 cards as your opening hand. Then take 1 water token from the supply.
walkthrough.image_caption: Ten starting cards laid out before shuffling

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Agents and Markers
walkthrough.text: Take all the components in your player color. Place 2 Agents on top of your Leader card. Set the third Agent, called the Swordmaster, off to the side. You can unlock it later by visiting the Swordmaster space. Place your score marker at 0 on the Victory Point track and your combat marker at 0 on the combat track. Place one cube on the 0 space of each of the four faction influence tracks.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player Agents, markers, and cubes in starting positions

walkthrough.title: Take Spies, Troops, and Control Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player also takes 3 Spy cylinders, 3 Control markers, and your remaining troop cubes. Place your troops in your garrison area next to your Leader. Spies and Control markers stay in front of you. You will place your Spies on observation posts around the board during the game, and Control markers go on locations you conquer in combat.
walkthrough.image_caption: Spies, troops, and Control markers ready for play

walkthrough.title: Deal Objectives and Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Deal 1 Objective card face up to each player. Each Objective shows a battle icon: Crysknife, Desert Mouse, or Ornithopter. The player whose Objective card has the first-player symbol goes first. Keep your Objective visible throughout the game because the battle icons on them matter for scoring when you win Conflicts. You are ready to play.
walkthrough.image_caption: Objective cards dealt to each player

summary.title: Choose the Board Side
bullets: Standard side for 1-4 players, expanded side for 5-6
Place centrally where everyone can reach

summary.title: Place the Shield Wall
bullets: Place Shield Wall token below Spice Refinery
Blocks sandworms at Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, and Imperial Basin

summary.title: Set Up Alliance Tokens
bullets: Place 1 Alliance token at level 4 on each faction track
First player to reach level 4 claims the token for 1 VP

summary.title: Build the Conflict Deck
bullets: Separate Conflict cards by tier (I, II, III)
Stack: 4x Conflict III on bottom, 5x Conflict II in middle, 1x Conflict I on top

summary.title: Prepare the Imperium Market
bullets: Shuffle 69-card Imperium deck
Deal 5 face-up cards as the Imperium Row

summary.title: Set Out Reserve and Intrigue Decks
bullets: Place Prepare the Way and The Spice Must Flow reserve stacks
Shuffle Intrigue deck and place face down

summary.title: Set Out Resource Tokens
bullets: Pile spice (orange), Solari (gold), and water (blue) tokens in the supply
Set Sandworm tokens and Maker Hook tokens nearby

summary.title: Choose Leaders
bullets: Each player picks a unique Leader (no same-House duplicates)
Leaders are two-sided with different abilities

summary.title: Take Your Starting Deck and Components
bullets: 10-card starting deck: 2 Convincing Argument, 2 Dagger, 1 Diplomacy, 2 Dune the Desert Planet, 1 Reconnaissance, 1 Seek Allies, 1 Signet Ring
Shuffle, draw 5 cards, take 1 water

summary.title: Set Up Your Agents and Markers
bullets: Place 2 Agents on Leader card, set Swordmaster aside
Score marker at 0, combat marker at 0, 1 cube on each faction track at 0

summary.title: Take Spies, Troops, and Control Markers
bullets: Take 3 Spy cylinders, 3 Control markers, remaining troops
Troops go in garrison; Spies and Control markers stay in front of you

summary.title: Deal Objectives and Determine First Player
bullets: Deal 1 Objective card face-up per player
First-player icon holder goes first; keep Objective visible for battle icon scoring

walkthrough.title: The Round Begins
walkthrough.text: Each round starts the same way. Flip the top card of the Conflict deck face up and place it where everyone can see it. This card shows what you are all fighting over this round, including victory point rewards, resources, and a battle icon. Then every player draws 5 cards from their personal deck. If your deck runs out, shuffle your discard pile to make a new deck, then keep drawing.
walkthrough.image_caption: Conflict card revealed at the start of a round

walkthrough.title: Agent Turn — Play a Card
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you have two choices: take an Agent turn or a Reveal turn. Let's start with the Agent turn, which is what you will do most often. Pick one card from your hand. Look at the icons in the top left corner. Those icons tell you which board spaces your Agent can visit. For example, if the card shows the Emperor icon, you can send your Agent to any Emperor-aligned space on the board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Card showing Agent icons that match board spaces

walkthrough.title: Agent Turn — Place Your Agent
walkthrough.text: Take one Agent from your Leader card and place it on a matching board space that is not already occupied by another player's Agent. Pay any costs the space requires. Some spaces cost Solari, water, or spice. Some require a minimum influence level on a faction track. Then resolve two things: the card's Agent box effect and the board space's reward. You can do these in any order.
walkthrough.image_caption: Agent placed on a board space with cost paid

walkthrough.title: Agent Turn — Deploy to Combat
walkthrough.text: If the board space you sent your Agent to has a Combat icon, something extra happens. You may deploy troops from your garrison to the Conflict area. You can send any troops you recruited this turn plus up to 2 from your garrison. Then advance your combat marker on the combat track. This signals to other players that you are entering the fight this round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Troops deployed from garrison to the Conflict area

walkthrough.title: Using Your Spies
walkthrough.text: Here is where Uprising gets interesting. You have 3 Spy cylinders that can be placed on observation posts around the board. When you want to visit a space that is already taken by an opponent, recall a Spy from a connected observation post. This is called Infiltrate, and it lets you ignore the blocking Agent. Alternatively, when you send an Agent to a space connected to one of your Spies, you can recall that Spy to draw 1 card. This is called Gather Intelligence. You cannot do both with the same Spy on the same turn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Spy on an observation post connected to a board space

walkthrough.title: Reveal Turn — Show Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Once you have placed all your Agents, or whenever you choose, you take a Reveal turn instead. Flip all your remaining hand cards face up. Each card has a Reveal box at the bottom. Resolve all those effects now. Sword icons add combat strength. Persuasion symbols give you buying power. Your total combat strength equals: troops in the Conflict times 2 each, plus sandworms times 3 each, plus sword icons from your revealed cards.
walkthrough.image_caption: Cards revealed showing sword icons and Persuasion values

walkthrough.title: Reveal Turn — Buy Cards
walkthrough.text: During your Reveal turn, spend your Persuasion to buy cards from the Imperium Row or the Reserve. Each card in the Row has a Persuasion cost in the top right corner. When you buy a card, it goes straight to your discard pile, not your hand. You will draw it in a future round. Quick heads up: unspent Persuasion is lost at the end of your Reveal turn. You cannot save it.
walkthrough.image_caption: Buying a card from the Imperium Row

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase
walkthrough.text: After everyone has taken their Reveal turn, combat begins. Starting with the first player, anyone with at least 1 unit in the Conflict can play Combat Intrigue cards or pass. Keep going around until everyone passes in a row. Then compare total combat strength. First place wins the Conflict card and its best reward. Second place gets the consolation reward. In games with 4 or more players, third place also gets a small reward. Quick heads up: if players tie for first, they each get second-place rewards and nobody takes the Conflict card.
walkthrough.image_caption: Combat strength comparison during the Combat phase

walkthrough.title: Sandworm Power
walkthrough.text: Sandworms are the signature mechanic of Uprising. To summon sandworms, you first need a Maker Hook token, which you can earn at Sietch Tabr or from certain card effects. Once you have a Maker Hook, specific board spaces and cards let you call sandworms into the Conflict. Each sandworm adds 3 combat strength. But the real power is reward doubling: if you have at least 1 sandworm in the Conflict when rewards are given out, all your rewards are doubled. That means a 2 victory point reward becomes 4 victory points. This is huge.
walkthrough.image_caption: Sandworm tokens in the Conflict area

walkthrough.title: After Combat — Cleanup
walkthrough.text: After combat resolves, all troops in the Conflict go back to the supply. Not your garrison, the supply. You will need to recruit them again before you can use them. Sandworms always return to the bank. Combat markers reset to zero. The winner may also place a Control marker if the Conflict was at Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, or Imperial Basin. Control gives you ongoing benefits when opponents visit that location.
walkthrough.image_caption: Troops returning to supply after combat

walkthrough.title: Makers Phase and Recall
walkthrough.text: After combat, check the spice-producing locations: Deep Desert, Hagga Basin, and Imperial Basin. If any of those spaces does not have an Agent on it, add 1 bonus spice from the bank to that space. This spice accumulates round after round, making unclaimed desert spaces more and more tempting. Finally, check if anyone has reached 10 victory points. If not, recall all Agents back to Leader cards, pass the first player marker clockwise, and start a new round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Bonus spice accumulating on unvisited desert spaces

walkthrough.title: Faction Influence and Alliances
walkthrough.text: Throughout the game, you will climb the four faction influence tracks. Every time you send an Agent to a faction-aligned space, you gain 1 influence with that faction automatically. Reaching level 2 earns you 1 victory point, but you lose it if you drop back below 2. Reaching level 4 lets you claim that faction's Alliance token for another victory point. Be careful though: if another player passes you on the track later, they steal your Alliance token and the victory point goes with it.
walkthrough.image_caption: Faction influence track showing level 2 and level 4 milestones

walkthrough.title: Deck Building and Trashing
walkthrough.text: Your deck starts with 10 basic cards, but it will grow as you buy cards from the Imperium Row and Reserve. When your draw pile is empty, shuffle your discard pile to form a new deck. Some card effects let you trash cards, which means removing them from the game permanently. Trashing your weaker starting cards like Daggers makes your deck stronger over time because you draw your best cards more often. Think of it as upgrading your toolbox.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player deck, discard pile, and trashed card area

walkthrough.title: Intrigue Cards
walkthrough.text: Intrigue cards are your secret weapons. You draw them from the Intrigue deck through board space rewards and card effects. Keep them hidden from other players. There are three types. Plot cards can be played during your Agent or Reveal turn for immediate effects. Combat cards can only be played during the Combat phase for extra strength or surprises. Endgame cards are saved until after the final round and played for bonus victory points during scoring.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three types of Intrigue cards: Plot, Combat, and Endgame

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends at the end of the round when any player reaches 10 or more victory points, or when the Conflict deck runs out. After the final round, all players may play their Endgame Intrigue cards for bonus victory points. Then total up all victory points from all sources: Conflict wins, battle icon pairs, faction influence, Alliance tokens, The Spice Must Flow cards, Intrigue cards, and any other card effects. Highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most spice wins. Then Solari, then water, then garrisoned troops.
walkthrough.image_caption: Victory Point track showing endgame scoring

walkthrough.title: Your Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: You have drawn 5 cards from your starting deck. Take a look at them. You will probably see some combination of Convincing Argument, Dagger, Diplomacy, Dune the Desert Planet, Reconnaissance, Seek Allies, and your Signet Ring. Each card shows Agent icons in the top left and effects in two boxes: the Agent box on top and the Reveal box on the bottom. For your first game, focus on the Agent icons. Those tell you where you can send your workers.
walkthrough.image_caption: Opening hand of 5 starting cards

walkthrough.title: Your First Agent Turn
walkthrough.text: Let's say you have a card with the Fremen icon, like Dune the Desert Planet. Play it face up in front of you. Now take one of your 2 Agents from your Leader card and place it on a Fremen space. A good opening move is Stillsuits, which gives you 1 water. Water is valuable because many powerful spaces and effects require it. Resolve the card's Agent box, then gain the board space reward.
walkthrough.image_caption: First Agent placed on Stillsuits space

walkthrough.title: Your Second Agent Turn
walkthrough.text: You have one Agent left. Look at your remaining 4 hand cards. Pick one that matches a space you want. If you have a card with the Landsraad icon, consider visiting the Swordmaster space to unlock your third Agent, though it costs 8 Solari, which you probably do not have yet. Instead, look for spaces that give you resources or troops. Playing your Signet Ring triggers your Leader's special ability, so that is always worth considering.
walkthrough.image_caption: Choosing the second Agent placement

walkthrough.title: Time to Reveal
walkthrough.text: With both Agents placed, you must take your Reveal turn. Flip your remaining 3 hand cards face up. Look at the Reveal boxes. Sword icons give you combat strength — each one adds 1 to your total. Persuasion symbols give you buying power for new cards. If you deployed any troops to the Conflict earlier, your combat strength is: number of troops times 2, plus sword icons from your revealed cards.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three cards revealed showing combat and Persuasion values

walkthrough.title: Buying Your First Card
walkthrough.text: Let's say your revealed cards give you 4 Persuasion total. Look at the 5 face-up cards in the Imperium Row. Cards costing 4 or less are available to you. Look for cards that match your strategy. Want to fight? Look for cards with sword icons in their Reveal box. Want resources? Look for strong Agent effects. Your purchased card goes to your discard pile. You will draw it in a future round when your deck reshuffles.
walkthrough.image_caption: Selecting a card from the Imperium Row

walkthrough.title: Your First Combat
walkthrough.text: If you sent troops to the Conflict, you are part of the fight. After everyone reveals, the Combat phase starts. If you have Combat Intrigue cards, you can play them now for extra strength. Then compare totals. For your first game, do not worry too much about winning every fight. The Conflict I card has modest rewards. Just get a feel for how combat strength adds up: troops times 2, sandworms times 3, and sword icons.
walkthrough.image_caption: First combat resolution with multiple players

walkthrough.title: Placing Your First Spy
walkthrough.text: At some point during the game, a card or board space will give you the chance to place a Spy. When it does, look at the observation posts on the board. These are the small circles connected to board spaces by dotted lines. Pick a space you think will be contested and place your Spy on a connected post. Next round, if an opponent takes that space before you, you can recall your Spy to Infiltrate and go there anyway.
walkthrough.image_caption: Spy placed on an observation post

walkthrough.title: Using Infiltrate
walkthrough.text: Let's say you want to visit a space but another player's Agent is already there. If you have a Spy on an observation post connected to that space, you can recall the Spy to Infiltrate. Remove the Spy from the board and place your Agent on the occupied space as normal. Your opponent's Agent stays — you are not removing it, you are just ignoring it. This is powerful for grabbing contested spaces like the Swordmaster or High Council.
walkthrough.image_caption: Recalling a Spy to Infiltrate an occupied space

walkthrough.title: Climbing Faction Tracks
walkthrough.text: Notice your faction influence markers on the four tracks. Every time you visit a faction space, you move up 1 on that track. Getting to level 2 is a big deal — it earns you 1 victory point. Getting to level 4 is even bigger because you claim that faction's Alliance token for another victory point. For your first game, try pushing one faction to level 2. Do not try to advance all four equally. Pick the faction that matches the board spaces you are already visiting.
walkthrough.image_caption: Influence marker advancing on a faction track

walkthrough.title: Getting a Maker Hook
walkthrough.text: If you pushed Fremen influence to at least level 2, you can visit Sietch Tabr. One of the rewards there is a Maker Hook token. This is a permanent upgrade that unlocks the ability to summon sandworms for the rest of the game. Grab one when you can, because sandworms are the most explosive mechanic in Uprising. Their reward-doubling ability can swing the game in a single round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Maker Hook token earned at Sietch Tabr

walkthrough.title: Summoning a Sandworm
walkthrough.text: With a Maker Hook in hand, you can summon sandworms through specific board spaces or cards. For example, the Deep Desert space lets you summon sandworms by paying water. When summoned, sandworms go directly to the Conflict area. They cannot be garrisoned. Each one adds 3 combat strength. But remember the Shield Wall — sandworms cannot join Conflicts at Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, or Imperial Basin unless someone has destroyed the Shield Wall.
walkthrough.image_caption: Sandworm token placed in the Conflict area

walkthrough.title: Sandworm Reward Doubling
walkthrough.text: Here is the payoff. Let's say the Conflict card awards 2 victory points, 2 spice, and 1 troop for first place. If you win with at least 1 sandworm in the Conflict, those rewards double: 4 victory points, 4 spice, and 2 troops. That is an enormous swing from a single round. Even second place with a sandworm can be worth more than first place without one. This is why getting Maker Hooks early is so valuable.
walkthrough.image_caption: Doubled rewards from sandworm presence

walkthrough.title: Battle Icons and Matching
walkthrough.text: When you win a Conflict, you take the Conflict card. Look at the battle icon on it: Crysknife, Desert Mouse, or Ornithopter. Now check your previously won Conflict cards and your Objective card. If any of them share the same battle icon, flip the matched pair face down for 1 bonus victory point. This matching mini-game adds up over the course of the game. Factor it into your combat decisions — sometimes it is worth fighting harder specifically to collect a matching icon.
walkthrough.image_caption: Matching battle icons on Conflict cards for bonus VP

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Decisions
walkthrough.text: By round 3 or 4, you should have a clear victory point path forming. Are you winning Conflicts consistently? Lean into combat with troops and sandworms. Are you buying powerful cards? Push for The Spice Must Flow purchases. Are you climbing faction tracks? Race for those Alliance tokens. The key is to commit to 1 or 2 paths and build your deck around them. Every card you buy should serve your strategy.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player board showing mid-game progress

walkthrough.title: Deck Thinning in Action
walkthrough.text: Your starting Daggers are weak. They provide minimal Agent value and only 1 sword on Reveal. When you see an opportunity to trash a card — through a board space effect or a purchased card's ability — get rid of your Daggers first. With 2 fewer bad cards in your deck of maybe 12 to 15 cards, the odds of drawing your powerful purchases go up significantly. Deck thinning is one of the most impactful things you can do in any deck-building game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Trashing a weak starting card to improve deck quality

walkthrough.title: The Late Game Push
walkthrough.text: As the game enters Conflict III territory, every round matters. The Conflict rewards are huge and victory point races tighten. Make sure you have troops in your garrison for these final battles. If you have been building toward sandworms, this is when the reward doubling becomes game-winning. If your strategy is economic, start buying The Spice Must Flow cards at 9 Persuasion each for guaranteed victory points. Keep an eye on your opponents' victory points — the game ends the moment someone hits 10.
walkthrough.image_caption: Late-game Conflict III card with high VP rewards

walkthrough.title: Endgame Intrigue Cards
walkthrough.text: If you have been collecting Endgame Intrigue cards throughout the game, now is when they pay off. After the final round ends, all players simultaneously play their Endgame Intrigue cards for bonus victory points. These can swing the final standings dramatically. A player who looked like they were in third place might jump to first with 2 or 3 well-timed Endgame cards. Then add up all victory points and crown the winner.
walkthrough.image_caption: Endgame Intrigue cards played for final VP

summary.title: Placing Your First Spy

summary.title: Using Infiltrate
bullets: Recall Spy from connected post to ignore opponent's Agent

summary.title: Climbing Faction Tracks
bullets: Visit faction spaces to gain +1 influence
Level 2 = 1 VP; Level 4 = Alliance token (+1 VP); specialize in 1-2 factions

summary.title: Getting a Maker Hook
bullets: Earn at Sietch Tabr (needs 2 Fremen influence)

summary.title: Summoning a Sandworm
bullets: Summoned via board spaces or cards (costs water)
Goes directly to Conflict — cannot be garrisoned
Blocked by Shield Wall at Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, Imperial Basin

summary.title: Sandworm Reward Doubling

Does not double Control placement or battle icon collection

summary.title: Battle Icons and Matching
bullets: Won Conflict cards show an icon: Crysknife, Desert Mouse, or Ornithopter
Match icons between Conflict cards + your Objective for 1 bonus VP per pair

summary.title: Mid-Game Decisions
bullets: Commit to 1-2 VP paths: combat, economy, or faction alliances
Every card purchase should serve your chosen strategy

summary.title: Deck Thinning in Action
bullets: Trash Daggers first — they are your weakest cards
Fewer bad draws = more powerful turns

bullets: Conflict III rewards are massive — have troops ready
Sandworm doubling or Spice Must Flow purchases close out games

summary.title: Endgame Intrigue Cards
bullets: Play after final round for bonus VP
Can dramatically swing final standings

walkthrough.title: Cards Have Two Lives
walkthrough.text: The most important concept in Dune Imperium is that every card serves two purposes. When you play it as an Agent, you use its Agent icons to visit a board space and get its Agent effect. When you save it for Reveal, you get its Reveal effect, which is usually combat strength or Persuasion. Every card you play as an Agent is one fewer card contributing to your combat and buying power. This tension is the soul of the game. Always ask yourself: do I need this card more as an Agent or on my Reveal turn?
walkthrough.image_caption: Card showing Agent box versus Reveal box

walkthrough.title: Agent Turns vs Reveal Turns
walkthrough.text: You take Agent turns first, placing workers one at a time. Once you choose to Reveal, you are done for the round. This means the order you place Agents matters. Place your most important Agent first, before opponents can block that space. If you are planning a big combat push, consider taking fewer Agent turns and Revealing with more cards in hand for maximum swords. Conversely, if the Conflict reward is weak, play all your cards as Agents for board space value.
walkthrough.image_caption: Decision between placing another Agent or Revealing early

walkthrough.title: Watch the Conflict Deck
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to how deep you are in the Conflict deck. Early Conflict I and II cards have modest rewards. Do not over-invest in winning them. Save your big pushes for Conflict III, where the rewards can be game-deciding. If you see 2 Conflict III cards left, start stockpiling troops and spice for the final battles.
walkthrough.image_caption: Conflict deck thinning as the game progresses

walkthrough.title: Spy Economy
walkthrough.text: You only have 3 Spies, and once you recall one, you need a new placement effect to put it back. Do not place all three early and burn them on Gather Intelligence draws. Instead, hold 1 or 2 Spies in reserve for high-impact Infiltrate plays. The best Spy targets are spaces that opponents frequently contest, like the Swordmaster space, High Council, or the strongest faction space for your strategy.
walkthrough.image_caption: Strategic Spy placement on high-value observation posts

walkthrough.title: Resource Conversion Chains
walkthrough.text: Spice is the most flexible resource. You can sell it for Solari at the Sell Melange space, and Solari pays for most powerful effects. Water is the scarcest resource, needed for desert locations and sandworm summoning. Build resource chains: harvest spice from desert spaces, sell some for Solari to fund expensive plays, and keep water flowing for sandworm opportunities. Do not let any single resource bottom out.
walkthrough.image_caption: Resource flow from spice to Solari to board actions

walkthrough.title: When to Buy The Spice Must Flow
walkthrough.text: The Spice Must Flow costs 9 Persuasion and gives 1 victory point when purchased. It is always available from the Reserve. In the late game, this becomes your primary victory point engine if you have built a high-Persuasion deck. Quick heads up: the card itself has no Agent icons, so it takes up a draw slot without helping your board presence. Do not buy it too early, or it dilutes your deck when you still need Agent flexibility.
walkthrough.image_caption: The Spice Must Flow reserve card

walkthrough.title: Control the Tempo
walkthrough.text: If you have 3 Agents and your opponent has 2, you get an extra turn to place before Revealing. This is a massive advantage. The Swordmaster costs 8 Solari to unlock, but the extra Agent turn often pays for itself within a round or two. Similarly, Revealing early with 3 or 4 cards in hand can be powerful when you need maximum combat strength for a critical Conflict.
walkthrough.image_caption: Third Agent providing extra board presence

walkthrough.title: Read Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Watch what your opponents are doing. If someone is pushing Fremen influence hard, they are probably going for sandworms. If someone bought the Swordmaster early, expect them to contest more board spaces. If someone has been hoarding Intrigue cards, expect a combat surprise or endgame victory point swing. Use your Spies to block their key spaces. Use your Agent placement order to force them into suboptimal moves. Dune is as much about reading the table as it is about building your own engine.
walkthrough.image_caption: Watching opponent board positions and influence tracks

summary.title: Cards Have Two Lives
bullets: Agent box for board placement; Reveal box for combat/Persuasion
Every card played as an Agent is one fewer for your Reveal turn

summary.title: Agent Turns vs Reveal Turns
bullets: Place most important Agent first before opponents block it
Fewer Agent turns = more Reveal cards = more combat strength

summary.title: Watch the Conflict Deck
bullets: Don't over-invest in Conflict I/II rewards
Save big pushes for Conflict III — game-deciding VP

summary.title: Spy Economy
bullets: Hold 1-2 Spies in reserve for high-impact Infiltrate plays
Best targets: Swordmaster, High Council, key faction spaces

summary.title: Resource Conversion Chains
bullets: Spice → sell for Solari → fund expensive plays
Keep water flowing for sandworm opportunities

summary.title: When to Buy The Spice Must Flow
bullets: Costs 9 Persuasion for 1 VP — strong late game, risky early
No Agent icons — dilutes your deck if bought too early

summary.title: Control the Tempo
bullets: 3 Agents vs 2 = major action advantage; prioritize Swordmaster
Reveal early with more cards when you need maximum combat strength

summary.title: Read Your Opponents
bullets: Track what factions and VP paths opponents are pursuing
Use Spies and placement order to block their key moves

walkthrough.title: Fremen Sandworm Engine
walkthrough.text: This is the premier combo in Uprising. Push Fremen influence to level 2 to unlock Sietch Tabr, grab a Maker Hook, and start summoning sandworms as early as round 3 or 4. If the opportunity arises, destroy the Shield Wall to unlock sandworm access to all Conflict locations. Once online, every Conflict win becomes a doubled reward. Pair this with cards that have strong Fremen Reveal effects for massive combat swings. The goal is to win 2 or 3 Conflicts with sandworms for a total of 6 to 8 victory points from combat alone.
walkthrough.image_caption: Fremen influence track with Maker Hook and sandworm tokens

walkthrough.title: Spacing Guild Heighliner Ambush
walkthrough.text: The Heighliner lets you deploy 5 troops for 6 spice. This is the single biggest combat swing in the game. The strategy: build a spice economy through desert harvesting, push Spacing Guild influence for access, and then drop 5 troops into a late-round Conflict after opponents have already committed their forces. The element of surprise is critical. If opponents know you have Heighliner access and 6 spice, they will overcommit to defend. Keep your spice total ambiguous if possible.
walkthrough.image_caption: Spacing Guild track with Heighliner deployment

walkthrough.title: Emperor Solari Engine
walkthrough.text: Emperor spaces give you Solari and access to Sardaukar elite troops. This strategy works as a flexible foundation. Build a Solari engine early, use it to fund expensive plays like the Swordmaster and High Council, and then pivot to either combat or The Spice Must Flow purchases in the mid-game. Emperor influence at level 2 also unlocks Imperial Privilege, one of the strongest board spaces. The Emperor path is rarely the flashiest, but it is consistently effective because Solari funds everything.
walkthrough.image_caption: Emperor influence track with Solari engine

walkthrough.title: Bene Gesserit Efficiency Play
walkthrough.text: The Bene Gesserit path rewards precision. Their spaces provide card draw, Intrigue cards, and influence manipulation. Pair this with aggressive deck thinning to create a lean, powerful deck. Your goal is to draw your best cards every round and accumulate Endgame Intrigue cards for a final victory point burst. This strategy often looks like it is losing until the final scoring, when 3 or 4 Endgame Intrigue cards suddenly add 4 to 6 victory points.
walkthrough.image_caption: Bene Gesserit influence track with Intrigue card collection

walkthrough.title: Feyd-Rautha and Built-in Trashing
walkthrough.text: Feyd-Rautha has a Leader ability that automatically trashes cards from your deck. This is arguably the strongest Leader in Uprising because deck thinning is so powerful, and he does it for free every round. With Feyd-Rautha, you can afford to buy more aggressively from the Imperium Row because your natural trashing keeps the deck lean. Pair him with a combat-heavy strategy to maximize the impact of consistently drawing your strongest sword cards.
walkthrough.image_caption: Feyd-Rautha Leader card with deck-thinning ability

walkthrough.title: The Bluffing Game
walkthrough.text: Combat in Dune Imperium has hidden information. You do not know your opponents' Reveal cards or Combat Intrigue cards until they are played. Use this to your advantage. Heavy troop deployment with weak Reveal cards can scare opponents into conceding a Conflict. Conversely, light troop deployment with strong hidden Reveal effects can catch an opponent off-guard. The Fremen faction is especially good at bluffing because many Fremen cards have strong sword Reveal effects that opponents cannot see coming.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hidden combat strength from unrevealed cards

walkthrough.title: Tempo Reads in the Opening
walkthrough.text: Your first two turns give you near-perfect information about your draws because you know your starting deck. Plan your opening sequence deliberately. For example: Turn 1 play Dune the Desert Planet to Stillsuits for water, Turn 2 play Seek Allies to a faction space for influence. Map out which card enables which Agent placement. After the opening, you will start buying cards and the deck becomes less predictable, so use these early rounds to establish your economic foundation.
walkthrough.image_caption: Opening turn sequence planning

walkthrough.title: Alliance Token Defense
walkthrough.text: Alliance tokens are worth 1 victory point each, but they can be stolen. If you are the first to reach level 4 on a faction track, every opponent who pushes past you steals your token and your victory point. Defend your Alliance by either building an insurmountable lead on a track that no one else is contesting, or by claiming Alliances on tracks where you are the only serious contender. In a 4-player game, ideally each player pushes a different faction. When 2 players contest the same faction, both waste resources.
walkthrough.image_caption: Alliance token contested between two players on a faction track

summary.title: Fremen Sandworm Engine
bullets: Push Fremen to 2+ → Sietch Tabr → Maker Hook → sandworms by round 3-4
Sandworm reward doubling can yield 6-8 VP from combat alone

summary.title: Spacing Guild Heighliner Ambush
bullets: Build spice economy, push Guild influence, drop 5 troops as surprise
Keep spice total ambiguous to maintain element of surprise

summary.title: Emperor Solari Engine
bullets: Solari funds Swordmaster, High Council, and late-game pivots
Level 2 unlocks Imperial Privilege — one of the strongest board spaces

summary.title: Bene Gesserit Efficiency Play
bullets: Deck thinning + Intrigue hoarding; looks behind until final scoring
3-4 Endgame Intrigue cards can add 4-6 VP in the last moment

summary.title: Feyd-Rautha and Built-in Trashing
bullets: Free deck thinning every round — arguably the strongest Leader
Pair with combat-heavy cards for consistently strong Reveal turns

summary.title: The Bluffing Game
bullets: Heavy troop deployment + weak Reveal = scare opponents into conceding
Light troops + hidden Fremen swords = surprise combat wins

summary.title: Tempo Reads in the Opening
bullets: Plan first 2 turns precisely — you know your starting deck
Establish economic foundation before deck becomes unpredictable

summary.title: Alliance Token Defense
bullets: Claim Alliances on uncontested tracks when possible
2 players contesting the same faction wastes resources for both

entries.definition: A turn where you play 1 card from your hand and send 1 Agent to a matching board space. You resolve the card's Agent box effect and the board space reward.
A turn where you flip all remaining hand cards face up, resolve their Reveal effects, set combat strength, and spend Persuasion to buy cards. Once you Reveal, you are done for the round.
Symbols in the top left of each card that show which board spaces your Agent can visit when you play that card on an Agent turn. Icons correspond to factions, Landsraad, City, and special categories.
The bottom section of a card showing effects that trigger when the card is revealed during your Reveal turn. Typically shows sword icons for combat strength or Persuasion for buying cards.

Your total fighting power during the Combat phase. Calculated as: troops times 2, plus sandworms times 3, plus sword icons from revealed cards, plus Combat Intrigue card bonuses. Must have at least 1 unit in the Conflict to contribute any strength.
A card revealed at the start of each round showing rewards for combat winners. Separated into tiers I, II, and III with escalating rewards. Shows a battle icon used for set-collection scoring.

Conflict I cards have modest rewards. Conflict II escalates. Conflict III has the highest VP and resource rewards in the game. The deck is stacked so Conflict I appears first and Conflict III appears last.

Three types: Crysknife, Desert Mouse, and Ornithopter. When you win a Conflict, match its battle icon with a previously won Conflict card or your Objective card to flip the pair face-down for 1 bonus VP.
Your personal troop reserve. Troops in garrison can be deployed to the Conflict during Agent turns at Combat spaces. Troops lost in combat return to supply, not garrison, and must be re-recruited.

Placed on Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, or Imperial Basin when you win a Conflict at that location. Opponents pay you an extra resource when they visit, and you get a free troop when the location's Conflict reappears.
A cylinder placed on observation posts around the board. Can be recalled to Infiltrate (ignore blocking Agent), Gather Intelligence (draw 1 card), or used passively with the Spy Agent icon on cards.
Small circular spaces connected to board spaces by dotted lines. Only one Spy per post. Your Spy on a post gives you options for the connected board space.
Recall a Spy from an observation post connected to an occupied board space to send your Agent there anyway. The opponent's Agent stays, but you ignore it. Powerful for grabbing contested spaces.
Recall a Spy from an observation post connected to a board space where you are placing your Agent. Draw 1 card from your deck. Cannot be combined with Infiltrate on the same Spy.
A special icon on some cards. When played, you may send your Agent to any board space connected to one of your Spies without recalling the Spy. The Spy stays in place.

A powerful one-time-use combat unit contributing 3 combat strength. Summoned via board spaces or card effects. Goes directly to the Conflict area and cannot be garrisoned. Returns to bank after combat.
A permanent token upgrade earned at Sietch Tabr or from certain effects. Required to summon sandworms. You keep it for the entire game and do not spend it when summoning.

When you have at least 1 sandworm in the Conflict, all your rewards from that combat are doubled. Applies to VP, spice, Solari, troops, water, and card draws. Does not double Control placement or battle icons.

A token blocking sandworm summoning at Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, and Imperial Basin. Can be permanently destroyed via Sietch Tabr's detonation icon or certain Intrigue cards. Destruction is irreversible.
One of four factions. Emperor spaces provide Solari and Sardaukar troops. Influence level 2 unlocks Imperial Privilege. Level 4 earns the Emperor Alliance token worth 1 VP.
One of four factions. Controls foldspace travel and the Heighliner, which deploys 5 troops for 6 spice. Influence level 2 unlocks Shipping. Level 4 earns the Guild Alliance token.
One of four factions. Provides card draw, Intrigue cards, and manipulation effects. Rewards efficiency and deck optimization. Level 4 earns the Bene Gesserit Alliance token.
One of four factions. Desert warriors with access to Sietch Tabr, Maker Hooks, Shield Wall destruction, and sandworm synergy. Level 4 earns the Fremen Alliance token.
Each faction has a track from 0 to 6. Advance by visiting faction spaces. Level 2 earns 1 VP (lost if you drop below). Level 4 earns the Alliance token. Some spaces require minimum influence.
Worth 1 VP. Earned by being the first player to reach level 4 on a faction influence track. Can be stolen if another player surpasses your influence level on that track.
Orange tokens. The most valuable resource on Arrakis. Harvested from desert spaces, used to pay costs, sell for Solari, and summon sandworms. Tiebreaker resource for final scoring.
Gold coin tokens. Money used to pay for board space costs, unlock the Swordmaster, buy High Council seats, and fund expensive plays. Earned from Emperor spaces and spice sales.
Blue tokens. The scarcest resource. Required for certain Arrakis actions and sandworm summoning. Each player starts with 1 water. Earned from Stillsuits and certain card effects.
Generated during your Reveal turn from card Reveal effects. Spent to buy cards from the Imperium Row or Reserve. Unspent Persuasion is lost at the end of your Reveal turn.
Five face-up cards from the Imperium deck available for purchase. When a card is bought, immediately refill from the deck. The Row is the main source of deck improvement.
Always-available cards: Prepare the Way (cheap utility) and The Spice Must Flow (9 Persuasion for 1 VP). Not limited by supply — you can buy as many as you can afford.
Permanently removing a card from your deck. Trashed cards are out of the game. Removes weak starting cards to improve average draw quality. One of the strongest mechanics in any deck-building game.
A Reserve card costing 9 Persuasion. Grants 1 VP when acquired. Has no Agent icons, so it dilutes your deck. Best purchased in the late game when VP matters more than Agent flexibility.
A Reserve card with a low Persuasion cost. Provides useful Agent icons and deck-thinning effects. A solid early-game purchase that helps smooth out your opening turns.
Intrigue card type playable during your Agent or Reveal turn. Provides immediate effects like gaining resources, troops, or influence. Use when the timing benefits you most.
Intrigue card type playable only during the Combat phase. Adds combat strength, deploys troops, or disrupts opponents. The surprise factor of hidden Combat Intrigue cards creates bluffing dynamics.
Intrigue card type played after the final round for bonus VP. Kept hidden until scoring. Accumulating several Endgame cards can swing the final standings by 4 to 6 VP.
Your third Agent, unlocked by visiting the Swordmaster board space and paying 8 Solari. Having 3 Agents versus opponents' 2 is a significant action advantage.
A powerful board space that provides ongoing benefits for the rest of the game. Costs Solari and is highly contested. A strong early-game investment.
A Fremen board space requiring 2 Fremen influence. Grants Maker Hook tokens and the option to destroy the Shield Wall. The gateway to the sandworm strategy.
A Spacing Guild board space that deploys 5 troops from supply to the Conflict for 6 spice. The biggest single combat swing in the game. Best used as a surprise late in the round.
A desert board space that produces spice and allows sandworm summoning. Accumulates bonus spice each round it goes unvisited via the Makers phase.
A board space where you sell spice for Solari. The exchange rate varies. A key link in the resource conversion chain from desert harvesting to funding expensive plays.
Asymmetric player power card. Two-sided with different abilities. Left side: main ability used during play. Right side: Signet Ring ability triggered when you play your Signet Ring card.
A starting card in every player's deck. When played on an Agent turn, it triggers your Leader's Signet Ring ability (right side of Leader card). A unique card that makes your Leader choice matter.
Dealt face-up at the start of the game. Determines first player and shows a battle icon used for matching with won Conflict cards. Keep visible throughout the game.
The race to 10 VP drives the game. Sources: Conflict wins, battle icon pairs, faction influence (level 2), Alliance tokens (level 4), The Spice Must Flow, card effects, and Endgame Intrigue.

An optional module adding contracts — small objectives that award resources or VP when completed. Contracts are taken from face-up slots and completed by visiting specified board spaces or meeting conditions.
Uses the expanded board side with additional spaces including Habbanya Erg. Adds more interaction, longer rounds, and the Makers phase checks one extra desert space. Supports team play variant.
